data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-10T01:39:29.065Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically around synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me recall what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported - they were always implemented as sync-over-async (blocking on async calls). 

In EF Core 9 (.NET 9), Microsoft started warning about this. In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), they made synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider throw by default, but you could opt back in by configuring `EnableSyncOverAsync()` or similar.


So if the user is currently on .NET 10 with that opt-in configured, moving to .NET 11 would likely remove that escape hatch entirely—the sync-over-async workaround would no longer be available, forcing them to refactor all their synchronous LINQ queries to async. change in .NET 11 would likely be the removal of that opt-in entirely, forcing all Cosmos DB operations to be async-only with no synchronous fallback. I should be clear about what I actually know versus what's speculative—.NET 11 hasn't released yet, so I'm basing this on the documented trajectory of EF Core's Cosmos provider deprecations across versions 8 and 9.

The logical progression Microsoft follows is warning → opt-in required → complete removal. Given that .NET 10 required an explicit opt-in for sync operations, .NET 11 would remove that configuration entirely, causing any synchronous LINQ calls like `ToList()`, `First()`, or `SaveChanges()` to throw `InvalidOperationException` at runtime. The only fix is converting all repository methods to async equivalents.
